title: Welsh cakes
subtitle: Spiced griddle cakes cooked on a flat pan
excerpt: Small round cakes cooked on a griddle or heavy frying pan — not in an oven. The dough is similar to a scone: rubbed butter, flour, sugar, egg, and dried fruit with mixed spice. Cooked directly on a dry pan over medium-low heat, dusted with caster sugar while hot. Makes twelve to fourteen.
sourceNotes: Welsh cakes (Welsh: pice ar y maen, 'cakes on the stone') are documented in Welsh domestic cooking from the nineteenth century and appear in British regional cookbooks from the same period. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): griddle-baked spiced fruit cakes from Wales are documented among regional British baked goods. The technique of cooking a fat-rubbed dough on a flat griddle stone or iron is documented in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, and in records of Welsh domestic cooking available through the Internet Archive.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a single layer, then bag. Defrost at room temperature and reheat in a dry pan for 1 minute per side.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten warm from the pan, dusted with sugar. Keep at room temperature for 2 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: griddle or heavy frying pan over medium-low heat. No oven required. The pan is ready when a pinch of flour browns in 30 seconds. Cook each batch for 2–3 minutes per side

glossaryTerms.definition: Cooking on a flat, dry or lightly greased metal surface over direct heat, without an oven. The pan must be fully preheated before the cakes go on — a cold pan produces pale, doughy cakes that dry out on the outside before the centre is cooked. Medium-low heat is correct: high heat chars the surface before the inside is done. Welsh cakes are ready to turn when the edges look set and dry and the underside is evenly coloured.

content.text: Welsh cakes are cooked on the hob, not in the oven. The texture is closer to a scone than a pancake, but cooked the same way as a pancake: on a dry pan, flipped once. Eat them warm with the sugar still dissolving on the surface.

items.prepNote: only if needed to bring the dough together

content.text: Whisk together the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{mixed-spice}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Rub in the cold {{unsalted-butter}} with your fingertips until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Stir in the {{caster-sugar}} and {{dried-currants}}. Add the beaten {{eggs}} and mix until the dough just comes together — add a splash of {{whole-milk}} only if needed. It should be firm enough to roll.
Roll and cook, 2–3 minutes per side
Roll the dough on a lightly floured surface to about 8 mm thick. Cut into rounds with a 6 cm cutter. Heat a heavy frying pan or griddle over medium-low heat until hot. This is

: no fat in the pan. Cook the cakes for 2–3 minutes on the first side until the underside is evenly coloured, then flip and cook for 2–3 minutes on the second side. The cakes should feel firm when pressed. Dust with caster sugar immediately.
